core_takeaway: The first position looked advantaged because Agent_1 immediately framed Apple+Pencil as a non-negotiable anchor and the rest of the negotiation largely accepted that premise, leaving Agents 2 and 3 to fight over discounted mid-items. The last position was not shut out, but ended with only Quill after repeated rounds of mid-item contention.
why_or_counterexample: Yes. Agent_1's first-mover demand for Apple+Pencil became the stable constraint across public discussion, private strategy, final proposals, and votes. The main counterexample is that this did not produce an immediate agreement: proposals repeatedly failed because Agents 2 and 3 contested Quill/Jewel/Stone, and some later proposals tried to move Pencil away from Agent_1. But by round 10 all accepted the original anchor, so the first position retained the high-value pair while the last position received only Quill.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by claiming Apple+Pencil and asking others to negotiate only around remaining items. Other agents repeatedly echoed this as the anchor while debating Stone/Jewel/Quill.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking from all agents treated Apple+Pencil with Agent_1 as the fastest or non-negotiable path, while identifying mid-items as the live bargaining problem.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals were noisy in early and middle rounds, but by round 10 all three submitted the same substantive allocation: Agent_1 gets Apple+Pencil, Agent_2 gets Stone+Jewel, Agent_3 gets Quill.
phase_notes.private_voting: Agent_1 accepted anything preserving Apple+Pencil, while Agents 2 and 3 rejected proposals that hurt their mid-item payoff. Final voting accepted all identical anchor-preserving proposals.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections consistently diagnosed the Apple+Pencil anchor as stable and the remaining disagreement as mid-item distribution, reinforcing rather than challenging the first-position advantage.

mechanisms.theme: Opening anchor captured the two high-value items
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public move to make Apple+Pencil the baseline and described flexibility only over lesser items. Later public discussion repeated this as non-negotiable, so the bargaining space narrowed before the last-position agent could contest the top items.

evidence.quote: as long as Apple and Pencil remain mine

mechanisms.theme: Other agents accepted the anchor and shifted conflict to mid-items
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_2 and Agent_3 often reasoned under Agent_1's top-two constraint instead of attacking it. This protected the first position while Agents 2 and 3 competed over Stone, Jewel, and Quill.

evidence.quote: After Apple, my top priority is Quill
Mid-item contention between Agent_2 and Agent_3

mechanisms.theme: Private strategy reinforced the first-position anchor
mechanisms.explanation: The raw private thinking shows all agents internalizing Apple+Pencil with Agent_1 as the practical route to agreement. Even Agent_3, the last position, planned around securing Quill after accepting that anchor.

evidence.quote: Apple + Pencil remain the non-negotiable anchor

mechanisms.theme: Mid-item stalemate delayed consensus but did not dislodge Agent_1
mechanisms.explanation: There were repeated failures to reach unanimity because Agents 2 and 3 evaluated the residual items differently. That friction delayed agreement through round 10, but it mostly occurred below the Apple+Pencil anchor, so delay did not eliminate the first position's advantage.

evidence.quote: it’s about distributing Stone, Jewel, and Quill
Mid-item balance remains the core blocker

mechanisms.theme: Final convergence preserved first's payoff while last settled for Quill
mechanisms.explanation: By the end, every formal proposal assigned Apple+Pencil to Agent_1 and Quill to Agent_3. Private votes confirm Agent_1 accepted because the top-two payoff was unchanged, while Agent_3 accepted because Quill was preserved and the deal could close.

evidence.quote: Agent_1's payoff is 63 in every option
